title.zh: 第四回 璟同折旗 蠻山夜語
title.en: Chapter 4: Jing Tong Breaks the Banner; Night-Speech in the Savage Hills

en: A banner breaks on savage ridges — the hero's soul blazes
By firelight brothers speak of death — those words outlast the night

passages.zh: 卻說九爐聯軍自東嶺紮營，已逾三月，糧道艱澀，士氣漸墮。璟同日日策馬巡壘，望蠻山諸峰而目光如淬。昭明設帳議事，展圖而嘆曰：「霧胤據險固守，以逸待勞，我軍若不奇襲其中樞，此役恐遷延至冬。」吳烈按劍不言，目色深沉。惟璟同起身，以指叩案三聲，笑道：「昭明兄所憂者，正璟同所欲解者。明日辰時，且看我去。」雲素華伏於帳角，以細筆默錄此語，心中已有不祥之感，卻不知所從何來。
passages.en: It is told that the allied armies of the Nine Furnaces had encamped along the Eastern Ridge for more than three months. Supply lines grew strained; morale began to sag like a weathered rope. Day after day Jing Tong rode circuits of the ramparts, his gaze fixed upon the peaks of the Savage Hills with the concentration of a blade being tempered. Zhao Ming convened the war-council, spread out his maps, and sighed: 'Wu Yin holds the high ground and waits at his ease while we exhaust ourselves. Unless we strike at his center by unconventional means, this campaign will drag into winter.' Wu Lie rested his hand on his sword-hilt and said nothing, his eyes deep as a well at midnight. Only Jing Tong rose from his seat, rapped the campaign table three times with his knuckle, and laughed: 'What Brother Zhao Ming fears is precisely what I mean to resolve. At the hour of the Dragon tomorrow — watch me go.' Yun Suhua crouched in the corner of the tent, recording these words in her fine script, and felt, without knowing its source, the cold shadow of an omen pass through her.

en: Ten thousand fathoms of savage mountain locked in cloud and smoke
Enemy ramparts tier on tier, iron armor linked in rows
Five thousand spears and halberds mirror the cold winter sun
Who dares ride alone into the face of that formation?

passages.zh: 翌晨，天色尚暗，璟同已甲冑齊備。其甲以玄鐵葉片鑲就，肩披虎皮，腰懸彎月刀，胯下青驄馬嘶鳴不絕，蹄踏黃土如擂戰鼓。吳烈親執轡繩相送，二人對視良久，無一語。璟同忽而大笑，奪韁而去，馬蹄聲急，如疾風掃落葉，轉瞬沒入晨霧之中。昭明立於吳烈身後，低聲曰：「此去若成，三軍士氣可振；若不成……」吳烈截其言道：「璟同不會不成。」語氣之篤定，似鑄鐵澆就，不容置疑。
passages.en: Before dawn the next morning, Jing Tong stood already in full armor. His suit was fashioned from plates of black iron, a tiger pelt draped across his shoulders, a crescent-moon blade hung at his waist, and his blue-roan stallion screamed and stamped the yellow earth like a war drum being beaten. Wu Lie personally held the bridle to see him off; the two men locked eyes for a long moment and neither spoke. Then Jing Tong threw his head back and laughed, snatched the reins, and was gone — hoofbeats rapid as a gust sweeping through fallen leaves, swallowed in an instant by the morning mist. Zhao Ming, standing behind Wu Lie, said quietly: 'If he succeeds in this, the spirit of the three armies will revive; if he does not —' Wu Lie cut him off. 'Jing Tong will not fail.' The certainty in his voice was cast iron, admitting no doubt.

redComment.zh: 昭明未竟之語，乃預言之半句耳。後文自補其缺，讀者宜記。
redComment.en: Zhao Ming's unfinished sentence is half a prophecy. Later chapters complete it; the reader should hold the silence here.

passages.zh: 霧中，璟同已穿越三道前哨。敵卒見一騎突來，尚未辨清旗號，已被其彎月刀橫掃落馬，血濺霜草。狼髑將軍者，霧胤麾下第一猛將，身長八尺，面刻狼牙紋，以骸骨為飾，曾一人力斬九爐先鋒二十餘騎，令聯軍聞名喪膽。是日，狼髑將軍正率本部精銳五千列陣於蠻山南麓，旌旗蔽日，刀槍如林。璟同自霧中現身，單騎當陣，以彎月刀遙指狼髑，朗聲大呼：「九爐璟同在此！狼髑鼠輩，可敢出陣一決？」
passages.en: Within the mist, Jing Tong had already passed through three forward pickets. Enemy soldiers, glimpsing a single rider burst upon them before they could read his banner, were swept from their saddles by the crescent blade, blood spattering the frost-stiff grass. The Wolf-Skull Commander was the foremost of Wu Yin's fighting captains: eight feet tall, his face tattooed with wolf-fang markings, adorned with bone ornaments, a man who had once cut down more than twenty of the Nine Furnaces vanguard riders single-handed, a name that made the allied forces pale. On this morning he led his five thousand élite troops in formation on the southern slope of the Savage Hills — banners blocking out the sun, blades and spears thick as a forest. Out of the mist Jing Tong emerged alone, a single rider facing the whole array, his crescent blade leveled from a distance at the Wolf-Skull Commander, his voice ringing out across the field: 'Jing Tong of the Nine Furnaces stands here! Wolf-Skull, you rat — dare you come out and settle this between us?'

en: A lone rider charges through smoke and points at the great war-banner
One arc of blade-light splits the freezing air
Five thousand armored soldiers hold their breath and stand still
Listening only to the wind lamenting on the savage ridge

passages.zh: 狼髑將軍怒目環視，冷笑一聲，拍馬而出。其坐騎乃黑花豹紋戰馬，蹄落地而塵飛數丈。二將相交，狼髑以開山大斧橫劈，風聲呼嘯；璟同以身側避，刀鋒貼耳而過，反手一斬，已割斷其左側馬鞍革帶。二騎錯鐙而過，各自回旋。狼髑訝然，目露輕蔑之色漸消，取而代之者，一絲難以察覺的駭意。三合之後，璟同佯敗退走，狼髑縱馬追之。璟同忽然急勒馬首，青驄馬後蹄人立，狼髑措手不及，衝勢未止，璟同彎月刀由下而上，以全身之力貫入，自狼髑左腋穿出，刀尖帶血，直指蒼天。狼髑將軍口噴鮮血，於馬上僵立片刻，轟然仰倒。
passages.en: The Wolf-Skull Commander swept a contemptuous glare across the field, gave a cold laugh, and spurred his horse forward. His mount was a black-dappled war stallion whose hoofbeats raised dust clouds yards high. The two warriors met: the Wolf-Skull swung his mountain-splitting axe in a horizontal arc, the air shrieking; Jing Tong twisted aside, the blade grazing past his ear, and with a back-handed slash cut through the leather girth-strap on his opponent's left side. The horses crossed stirrups and wheeled away. Surprise flickered in the Wolf-Skull's eyes — the contempt draining out of them, replaced by something barely perceptible: the first grain of fear. After three exchanges, Jing Tong feigned defeat and pulled back; the Wolf-Skull drove his horse in pursuit. Then Jing Tong wrenched his reins sharply; the blue-roan reared up on its hind legs. The Wolf-Skull, his momentum unspent, could not check himself in time — and Jing Tong drove the crescent blade upward with the full force of his body, entering below the left armpit and emerging above it, the tip blood-bright, pointing at the open sky. The Wolf-Skull Commander spouted crimson, sat rigid in his saddle for a single suspended moment, then crashed backward to the earth.

passages.zh: 璟同提刀縱馬，馳入敵陣，揮刀斬落敵軍大纛。旗杆中斷，纛旗墮地，五千敵卒驟然失聲，繼而如山崩般潰散。昭明已在後陣等候，見纛旗傾倒，立命諸軍擂鼓齊進。九爐聯軍吶喊之聲震動蠻山，積雪從山巔滑落，如素縞垂掛。此役，璟同以一騎之力，當五千之眾，斬主將，折大纛，破敵線，盡收蠻山南麓諸隘。雲素華於中軍高處遠觀，執筆而手微顫，所書之字，筆觸較平日深重三分。
passages.en: Jing Tong raised his dripping blade and rode into the enemy formation, slashing down the great war-banner with a single stroke. The flagpole snapped; the banner crumpled to the ground. Five thousand enemy soldiers fell suddenly silent — then broke apart like a collapsing mountain. Zhao Ming, waiting in the rear formation, saw the banner fall and immediately ordered every drum in the army beaten and the full advance. The war-cry of the Nine Furnaces alliance shook the Savage Hills, and snow slid from the peaks in long white sheets like mourning silk unfurling. In this engagement, Jing Tong with a single horse faced five thousand troops, slew the enemy commander, broke the banner, shattered the enemy line, and recovered all the passes on the southern slopes of the Savage Hills. Yun Suhua watched from a height in the central command, her brush-hand trembling slightly; the characters she wrote that day pressed three shades deeper into the paper than usual.

passages.zh: 夜，九爐諸將大宴於蠻山頂，篝火連天，酒盈百甕。璟同血跡甫洗，白衣披身，坐於吳烈之側，二人遠離酒席喧囂，獨對一堆篝火。山風冷冽，松濤聲聲，火光將二人面容映得忽明忽暗。璟同以樹枝撥弄火苗，忽問曰：「吳烈，你怕死麼？」吳烈沉吟片刻，仰首望星，答曰：「我從未遇見過死。何必懼怕一個陌生人？」璟同聞言，久久不語，最後低笑道：「好一個陌生人。」
passages.en: That night, the generals of the Nine Furnaces held a great feast on the summit of the Savage Hills — bonfire light reaching toward the sky, wine filling a hundred jars. Jing Tong, his bloodstains barely washed away, had draped a white outer robe over himself and sat beside Wu Lie; the two had drifted away from the noise of the banquet and now faced a single fire alone. The mountain wind was sharp and cold, the sound of pine-boughs constant, the firelight making their faces shift between brightness and shadow. Jing Tong poked at the flames with a branch, then asked suddenly: 'Wu Lie — are you afraid of death?' Wu Lie was silent for a moment, tilting his head back to look at the stars, then answered: 'I have never met it. Why should I fear a stranger?' Jing Tong heard this and was quiet for a long while. At last he laughed softly and said: 'What a stranger.'

redComment.zh: 吳烈此語，豪氣干雲，然讀者自後文觀之，方知此乃最沉痛之反語。死者非陌生人——死者，已然識他久矣。雲素華聞此言，筆下暗記四字：「它識他矣。」
redComment.en: Wu Lie's words ring with magnificent bravado; yet read backward from what follows, they become the most agonizing irony in the book. Death is no stranger — death has long known him. Yun Suhua, hearing this, privately wrote four words in her journal: It has met him.

passages.zh: 火光漸低，二人皆無睡意。吳烈取出隨身竹簡，磨墨展紙，對璟同道：「今日大捷，當留詩以記之。你我各賦律詩一首，互為唱和，如何？」璟同拊掌：「正合我意。你先。」
passages.en: The fire burned lower; neither man felt any inclination toward sleep. Wu Lie drew out the bamboo writing-tablet he carried at his belt, ground his ink, spread his paper, and said to Jing Tong: 'Today's great victory deserves a poem to mark it. Let each of us compose a regulated verse and answer the other's — what do you say?' Jing Tong clapped his hands: 'Exactly my thought. You go first.'

en: Savage ridge — autumn frost — the spirit in one's bones runs cold
Where the lone banner breaks, the light of blood lingers still
Who can stand against a man who rides into formation asking for rivals?
Galloping into the wind he laughs at his own gladness
Victory and defeat have always been a single dream
Glory and achievement at this moment are worth a snap of the fingers
By firelight, drinking together, we must drain the cup to the dregs
Tomorrow the rivers and mountains will offer their dangers again
One rider charges through the clouds into ten thousand halberds
The great banner falls — the drumbeats multiply
A hero does not ask about what came before his life
Brothers look at each other: between them flows a river of spirit
Nightfire lights the mountain — the mountain's color is ancient
Cold stars reflect in water — the water's sound is a song
If this body could grow old together with heaven and earth
Lying drunk on the Savage Hills — even that would be time well wasted

passages.zh: 二詩既成，互相傳閱，各自頷首而笑。璟同忽斂笑容，以手按吳烈之肩，低聲道：「吳烈，你我之約，生死不棄，可還記得？」吳烈回握其臂，道：「自然記得。」璟同點頭，目光越過篝火，遠視山下燈火如繁星散落，沉默良久，方道：「記得便好。」雲素華蟄伏於數丈之外的暗處，以最細微之筆觸，將此夜一切默錄於案。她於「記得便好」四字之後，另起一行，以極小字寫道：「死已識君，正在等候。」旋即以指蹭去，然痕跡猶存。
passages.en: When both poems were complete, they passed the pages to each other, each nodding with a smile. Then Jing Tong let his smile fade; he placed his hand on Wu Lie's shoulder and said quietly: 'Wu Lie — our oath, never to abandon each other in life or death: you remember it, don't you?' Wu Lie gripped his arm in return. 'Of course I remember.' Jing Tong nodded, his gaze passing over the bonfire to rest on the scattered lights in the valley below, like stars brought to earth, and after a long silence he said: 'Good that you remember.' Yun Suhua crouched in the darkness several yards away, recording every moment of this night in the finest possible script. After the four characters 'good that you remember,' she began a new line and wrote, in characters so small they were nearly invisible: 'Death has met him already. It is patient.' Then she rubbed the line out with her fingertip. But the impression remained in the paper.

redComment.zh: 此批乃全書膽魄所在。雲素華非僅書記，乃命運之見證者。她所蹭去之字，即此書之魂——消去而不滅，如璟同之於吳烈，死而不散。
redComment.en: This note is the nerve-center of the entire book. Yun Suhua is not merely a scribe but a witness to fate. The words she rubbed away are the soul of the narrative — erased but not destroyed, as Jing Tong will be to Wu Lie: dead, but not dispersed.

passages.zh: 夜深，諸將漸散，篝火衰微如老人之息。璟同枕臂而臥於地，頃刻鼾聲微起。吳烈獨坐，不眠，以手指蘸殘酒，在岩石上一筆一畫，寫了又抹，抹了又寫，旁人不知其所書何字。山風過處，松針落於他發間，他亦不覺。雲素華悄然收拾筆墨，回望此二人一眼——一臥一坐，火光將其影子拖長至山崖邊緣——心中默念：此夜當記，此夜當記。
passages.en: The night deepened; the generals drifted away one by one; the bonfires shrank to the shallow breathing of old men. Jing Tong lay down with his arm as pillow and in moments was quietly snoring. Wu Lie sat alone, sleepless, dipping one finger in the dregs of his wine and writing on the surface of a rock — writing and erasing, erasing and writing — and no one could see what characters he made. The mountain wind passed through; pine needles fell into his hair and he did not notice. Yun Suhua quietly gathered her brushes and ink, and glanced back once at the two men — one lying, one sitting, the firelight stretching their shadows to the very edge of the cliff — and repeated silently to herself: this night must be recorded. This night must be recorded.

en: When beacon fires go out, the mountain's color ages
When heroes fall asleep drunk, their dreaming souls grow weightless
They do not know that there where the bonfire-shadows waver
The west wind has already come to know this feeling

passages.zh: 是夜，蠻山收復，九爐聯軍士氣大振，昭明已遣快馬報捷於後方。然霧胤龜縮深山，玉璽天命印仍不知所蹤。次日黎明，斥候來報，霧胤已撤往北嶺更高處，以百里縱深之險地為新壁壘，誓與聯軍相持。昭明展圖，眉頭深鎖。而吳烈，已在蠻山頂立足，負手望北，眼中是山是雲，還是別的什麼，無人能辨。璟同立於其側，大笑曰：「再難又如何？昨日那樣的仗，你我再打一次便是。」吳烈未答，唯側首看他片刻，而後亦笑——笑得無比溫柔，無比沉重。雲素華在遠處，一字不漏，盡數錄入。
passages.en: That night, with the Savage Hills recovered and the spirit of the Nine Furnaces armies revived, Zhao Ming had already dispatched fast riders to carry the news of victory to the rear. Yet Wu Yin had retreated deep into the mountains; the jade Mandate Seal was still unaccounted for. At dawn the next day, scouts reported that Wu Yin had pulled back to higher ground on the Northern Ridge, establishing a new stronghold in a hundred li of mountain depth, vowing to outlast the alliance in a prolonged stand. Zhao Ming spread his maps and his brow furrowed. Wu Lie stood at the summit of the Savage Hills, hands clasped behind him, looking north — whether what his eyes held was mountain, or cloud, or something else entirely, no one could say. Jing Tong stood at his side and laughed out loud: 'What of it, however hard it gets? A fight like yesterday's — you and I will just fight one more.' Wu Lie did not answer. He only turned his head and looked at him for a moment, then smiled as well — a smile of unbearable gentleness, of unbearable weight. Yun Suhua, at her distance, did not miss a single word. She recorded all of it.

passages.zh: 後人有詩讚璟同蠻山之役曰：「一人當關，五千膽落；折旗所處，英名不磨。」然雲素華之記錄，於此讚語之後，獨留空白一行，不置一字。讀者或問：此行何意？或有識者答曰：空白者，非無字，乃留與將來之哀慟，預先佔位也。此乃第四回終。
passages.en: Later generations composed a verse in praise of Jing Tong's campaign at the Savage Hills: 'One man held the pass; five thousand lost their nerve. Where the banner broke, his name will not wear away.' Yet in Yun Suhua's record, after this praise, she left a single blank line — not one character written there. Readers have wondered at it. Those who understood have answered: the blank is not empty. It is space reserved in advance for a grief not yet arrived. Here ends the fourth chapter.
